stem: A 38-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder has been stable on lithium carbonate for five years following two manic admissions in her late twenties. She presents to your community mental health clinic reporting six months of progressive fatigue, cold intolerance, mild constipation, and three kilograms of weight gain despite unchanged appetite. Her mood has remained euthymic and she denies depressive symptoms or suicidal ideation. She works full time as a primary school teacher and is keen to remain on her current mood stabiliser given the prior severity of her relapses. She is not pregnant and uses an intrauterine device.

data_table.Examination: Dry skin, no palpable goitre, no proximal weakness, mood euthymic
data_table.Investigations: Lithium level 0.7 mmol/L (12 hour trough); TSH 12.4 mIU/L; free T4 8.5 pmol/L; eGFR 84 mL/min/1.73m2; calcium 2.38 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in her management?

options.text: Continue lithium at current dose and commence thyroxine 50 mcg daily with TFTs in six weeks
options.rationale: Lithium-induced overt hypothyroidism is treated with thyroxine replacement while continuing the mood stabiliser, with TFT review at six weeks to titrate dose toward a euthyroid TSH. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG 2020)

options.text: Cease lithium immediately and switch to sodium valproate 500 mg twice daily indefinitely
options.rationale: Abrupt lithium cessation carries a high early relapse risk in bipolar I disorder and is not indicated for treatable lithium-induced hypothyroidism; valproate is teratogenic in a woman of reproductive age. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG 2020)

options.text: Reduce lithium to 400 mg nocte to permit endogenous thyroid recovery and recheck TFTs at four weeks
options.rationale: Lithium-induced hypothyroidism is dose-independent at therapeutic levels and dose reduction does not reliably restore thyroid function while it risks subtherapeutic mood-stabiliser cover. (Source: Australian Medicines Handbook 2025: lithium)

options.text: Continue lithium and refer urgently for radioiodine ablation of presumed autoimmune thyroiditis
options.rationale: Radioiodine ablation treats hyperthyroidism, not hypothyroidism, and is the wrong direction of therapy for a low free T4 with raised TSH. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology, hypothyroidism)

options.text: Continue lithium and commence carbimazole 5 mg daily empirically pending antibody results
options.rationale: Carbimazole is an antithyroid drug indicated for hyperthyroidism and would worsen the existing biochemical hypothyroidism. (Source: Australian Medicines Handbook 2025: carbimazole)

explanation.summary: Lithium causes hypothyroidism in 5 to 35 percent of long-term users via blocked thyroid hormone release and impaired iodine organification, with women and patients with positive TPO antibodies at highest risk. RANZCP recommends six-monthly TSH for the first year and then annually, plus calcium and eGFR. Lithium-induced hypothyroidism is treated with thyroxine while continuing lithium, because switching mood stabilisers carries higher relapse risk than concurrent thyroid replacement. Abrupt lithium cessation risks early bipolar relapse and is reserved for toxicity, severe renal impairment, or pregnancy planning.
key_points: TSH, calcium and eGFR are checked six-monthly in year one then annually on lithium
Lithium-induced hypothyroidism is treated with thyroxine, not lithium cessation
Bipolar relapse risk after abrupt lithium cessation is highest in the first three months
Women and TPO-positive patients carry the highest hypothyroidism risk on lithium
explanation.pearls: Start thyroxine 50 mcg daily for an overt lithium-induced hypothyroidism with TSH above 10 mIU/L and recheck TFTs six weeks later, titrating to TSH 0.5 to 2.5 mIU/L without altering the lithium dose.

stem: A 62-year-old man with bipolar I disorder has taken lithium carbonate for twenty-eight years. He attends your community mental health clinic for annual review. He has had no mood relapses in seven years and his lithium level has been stable between 0.6 and 0.8 mmol/L. Over the past three years his eGFR has fallen from 72 to 48 mL/min/1.73m2 on consecutive annual checks. He has hypertension treated with amlodipine, takes no NSAIDs, and his urine albumin to creatinine ratio is 1.4 mg/mmol. His mother required dialysis in her late seventies from diabetic nephropathy. He is highly anxious about losing the mood stabiliser that has kept him well.

data_table.Examination: Euthymic, no tremor, no oedema, lying-standing BP without postural drop
data_table.Investigations: Lithium 0.74 mmol/L; eGFR 48 mL/min/1.73m2; creatinine 138 micromol/L; uACR 1.4 mg/mmol; TSH 2.1 mIU/L; calcium 2.42 mmol/L
data_table.Medications: Lithium carbonate 750 mg nocte; amlodipine 5 mg daily

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management of his lithium therapy at this stage?

options.text: Cease lithium immediately and switch to olanzapine 10 mg nocte as the new long-term mood stabiliser
options.rationale: Abrupt cessation of lithium after decades risks early bipolar relapse and there is no evidence olanzapine monotherapy matches lithium for relapse prevention in bipolar I disorder. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic, bipolar disorder)

options.text: Continue lithium at the current dose and arrange shared care with a nephrologist plus six-monthly eGFR review
options.rationale: Lithium can be continued in mild to moderate CKD when it remains the most effective mood stabiliser, with nephrology co-management and at least six-monthly eGFR, lithium level and calcium monitoring. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG 2020)

options.text: Continue lithium and commence ramipril 2.5 mg daily for renoprotection despite normal urinary albumin
options.rationale: ACE inhibitors reduce renal lithium clearance and can precipitate toxicity; routine ACE inhibitor initiation is not indicated without proteinuria and a clear renoprotective target. (Source: KHA-CARI Guidelines: CKD management)

options.text: Continue lithium and add a thiazide diuretic to treat the presumed lithium-induced nephrogenic diabetes insipidus
options.rationale: Thiazides reduce renal lithium clearance and predictably raise serum lithium, precipitating toxicity, and are not first-line empirical therapy without polyuria documented. (Source: Australian Medicines Handbook 2025: lithium)

options.text: Reduce lithium to 250 mg nocte to halt the decline in eGFR and accept a likely subtherapeutic level
options.rationale: Subtherapeutic lithium loses prophylactic efficacy and lithium-related CKD progresses slowly even at therapeutic levels, so an arbitrary dose cut sacrifices benefit without proven renal gain. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG 2020)

explanation.summary: Long-term lithium causes chronic interstitial nephritis with a slow eGFR decline averaging around 0.5 to 2 mL/min/1.73m2 per year, accelerated by repeated toxicity episodes and concurrent nephrotoxins. RANZCP guidance allows continuation through CKD stage 3 when lithium remains the most effective option and the patient is well counselled, with nephrology shared care and tightened monitoring. ACE inhibitors, thiazides and NSAIDs all raise lithium levels and should be introduced cautiously with closer level checks. The trigger to switch is end-stage renal disease, repeated toxicity, or loss of mood efficacy, not a single eGFR threshold in isolation.
key_points: Lithium-related CKD is slowly progressive; abrupt cessation rarely halts it
Bipolar relapse risk after lithium cessation peaks in the first three months
ACE inhibitors, thiazides and NSAIDs raise serum lithium levels
Plan shared care with nephrology when eGFR falls below 60 mL/min/1.73m2
explanation.pearls: When eGFR falls below 60 mL/min/1.73m2 on lithium, move to six-monthly lithium levels and eGFR, engage nephrology shared care, and reserve a switch for end-stage renal disease, repeated toxicity, or loss of mood efficacy rather than a single number.
